title: Supabase API tokens should not be disclosed
description: In most cases, trust boundaries are violated when a secret is exposed in a source code repository or an uncontrolled deployment environment. Unintended people who don’t need to know the secret might get access to it. They might then be able to use it to gain unwanted access to associated services or resources.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

checks.message: Supabase API token (JWT) detected in source code. Supabase tokens are JSON Web Tokens (JWTs) that provide authentication and authorization for Supabase backend-as-a-service platform built on PostgreSQL. These tokens grant access to your Supabase project including: database operations (SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) on PostgreSQL tables, real-time subscriptions to database changes, authentication services managing user accounts and sessions, storage bucket operations for file uploads and downloads, edge functions and serverless compute resources, Row Level Security (RLS) policy evaluation, API endpoints for RESTful and GraphQL queries, and administrative operations depending on token type. Supabase uses two primary token types: ANON (anonymous) keys intended for client-side applications with limited permissions controlled by RLS policies, and SERVICE_ROLE keys providing full administrative access bypassing all RLS policies with unrestricted database privileges. Exposing Supabase tokens allows attackers to: access and exfiltrate sensitive data from all database tables (SERVICE_ROLE bypasses RLS, ANON may access public data or data permitted by misconfigured RLS), modify or delete database records including user accounts and critical business data, create fake user accounts or hijack existing authentication sessions, upload malicious files to storage buckets consuming quota and hosting malware, execute arbitrary SQL through exposed functions or edge functions, subscribe to real-time database updates revealing business activity patterns, enumerate database schema discovering table structures and relationships, abuse API rate limits and quota causing service disruption, incur substantial usage charges through excessive compute and storage operations, manipulate authentication flows to bypass security controls, access environment variables and secrets in edge functions, and potentially pivot to other connected services through compromised backend. The impact severity depends on token type: SERVICE_ROLE keys provide CRITICAL full administrative access equivalent to database superuser, while ANON keys provide HIGH risk limited by RLS policies but still dangerous if policies are misconfigured or absent. For SERVICE_ROLE compromises: attackers gain complete control over your Supabase project including ability to drop tables, modify schemas, access all data regardless of RLS, steal user credentials and PII, modify authentication settings, delete backups, change project settings, and potentially lock you out of your own project. For ANON key compromises: attackers can exploit RLS policy gaps, access public or insufficiently protected data, create spam accounts consuming resources, upload files exhausting storage quota, trigger edge functions consuming compute credits, enumerate public API endpoints, perform credential stuffing attacks, and analyze application behavior through API interactions. Financial impact includes unexpected bills from: excessive storage consumption through malicious file uploads, compute charges from abusive edge function invocations, bandwidth overage from data exfiltration or DDoS-style API requests, and Premium plan costs if attackers upgrade your project tier. Security impact extends to: data breaches exposing customer PII potentially violating GDPR, CCPA, or HIPAA; reputational damage from service abuse or data leaks; compliance violations from inadequate secrets management; loss of customer trust if authentication is compromised; and potential legal liability from exposed sensitive data. IMMEDIATE ACTIONS REQUIRED: 1) CRITICAL: Revoke compromised token immediately in Supabase dashboard (Project Settings → API → Generate new keys), distinguish carefully between revoking ANON vs SERVICE_ROLE keys as each has different regeneration procedures; 2) Generate new tokens with proper segregation: create fresh SERVICE_ROLE key for backend services only, generate new ANON key for client applications with strict RLS; 3) Rotate all dependent secrets that may have been exposed alongside the token (database passwords, storage credentials, third-party API keys); 4) Audit database activity during exposure window: review Supabase logs for unauthorized queries, check '_supabase_admin' schema for suspicious activity, examine auth.users table for fake accounts or modifications, review storage buckets for malicious file uploads, inspect edge function invocation logs, audit real-time subscription patterns, check for schema changes or RLS policy modifications, review API usage metrics for anomalies; 5) Immediate data protection actions: enable Row Level Security on all tables if not already active, review and tighten RLS policies to principle of least privilege, audit table permissions and column-level security, check for data exfiltration in access logs, verify backup integrity and retention, enable Supabase database replication for disaster recovery; 6) Update all applications: replace exposed tokens in backend services (use environment variables, never .env files in repos), update mobile and web applications with new ANON key (requires app redeployment), notify users if authentication may have been compromised, force password resets if user accounts were accessed, review all API client configurations; 7) Implement secure token management: store SERVICE_ROLE keys ONLY in backend environments using secrets managers (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, Azure Key Vault, GCP Secret Manager, Kubernetes secrets with RBAC), NEVER expose SERVICE_ROLE keys in client-side code or Git repositories, use ANON keys in frontend applications as intended with proper RLS, implement token rotation schedule (quarterly minimum), maintain separate tokens for different environments (development, staging, production), document token ownership and usage, implement least privilege: use ANON keys wherever possible, create custom JWT tokens with restricted claims for specific use cases, leverage Supabase service_role sparingly and only server-side; 8) Security hardening: enable MFA on Supabase account to prevent dashboard compromise, implement IP allowlisting in Supabase project settings if static IPs available, configure rate limiting and abuse prevention in edge functions, enable database activity monitoring and alerting, set up Supabase log streaming to SIEM for correlation, implement anomaly detection on API usage patterns, enable storage bucket access logging, configure webhook notifications for unusual activity; 9) RLS policy review and hardening: audit ALL tables for RLS enablement, review policy logic for authentication checks (auth.uid()), test policies with various authentication states, implement policy testing in CI/CD pipeline, enable RLS on new tables by default, restrict public access to read-only where possible, implement audit logging within RLS policies, document policy intent and security model; 10) Application-level protection: implement secondary authentication checks in application code, validate JWT signatures locally before database calls, enforce business logic constraints beyond RLS, implement request rate limiting at application layer, log and monitor all database operations, use Supabase client-side libraries with proper configuration, implement Content Security Policy preventing token exfiltration, audit third-party dependencies for vulnerabilities; 11) Monitoring and detection: set up alerts for: API usage exceeding baselines, unexpected geographic request origins, failed authentication attempts spike, new user registration surges, storage quota approaching limits, edge function invocations outside business hours, database query patterns deviating from normal, real-time subscription count anomalies; configure Supabase webhooks for administrative events, integrate Supabase logs with security monitoring tools, implement synthetic monitoring to detect service abuse, establish baseline metrics for anomaly detection; 12) Infrastructure security: review database connection security, ensure SSL/TLS for all Supabase connections, audit network policies and firewall rules, verify backup encryption and retention policies, enable database point-in-time recovery, implement infrastructure as code for Supabase configuration, maintain separate projects for different security zones; 13) Incident response: document this exposure in security incident log, conduct post-incident review identifying root cause, update secrets management procedures, conduct team training on Supabase security best practices, review and update incident response playbook, notify affected users if required by data protection regulations, prepare for potential regulatory disclosures if PII was exposed; 14) Compliance and governance: review compliance with SOC 2, PCI DSS, HIPAA, GDPR, CCPA as applicable, update risk register with this incident, conduct security audit of Supabase usage across organization, implement mandatory code review for Supabase integration, establish Supabase security baseline and standards, conduct regular penetration testing of Supabase-backed applications; 15) Long-term prevention: implement pre-commit hooks scanning for Supabase tokens, use git-secrets or truffleHog to prevent token commits, conduct regular secret scanning of repositories, implement secrets rotation automation, establish Supabase security champions program, create secure Supabase development guidelines, maintain inventory of all Supabase projects and tokens, conduct quarterly access reviews, implement just-in-time access for SERVICE_ROLE keys. Remember: SERVICE_ROLE keys should NEVER be used in frontend code, committed to Git, embedded in mobile apps, shared via email/Slack, stored in browser localStorage, included in screenshots/documentation, or shared with untrusted parties. Treat SERVICE_ROLE keys with same security as database superuser passwords. For additional guidance: review Supabase security documentation at supabase.com/docs/guides/platform/security, implement Supabase Security Checklist, review Row Level Security best practices, and consider Supabase security audit services for critical applications.
